statement: For measles in a fully susceptible population, the basic reproduction number is commonly placed in the high-transmission range around 12 to 18. A Normal prior with mean 15 and standard deviation 2.5 captures this canonical range while assigning negligible probability to nonpositive values; the variable is dimensionless and interpreted as expected secondary infections per primary infectious case.
source: Anderson and May, Infectious Diseases of Humans: Dynamics and Control

statement: For seasonal influenza in a broadly susceptible population, empirical reviews usually place R0 near 1.2 to 1.5. A LogNormal prior with natural-log median about 1.3 and moderate spread is a practical positive prior for transmission modeling; mu and sigma are on the natural-log scale.
source: Biggerstaff et al., systematic review of influenza reproduction numbers, BMC Infectious Diseases, 2014

statement: For the ancestral SARS-CoV-2 virus before widespread immunity and control measures, early outbreak estimates often centered R0 around 2 to 3. A LogNormal prior with median about 2.6 and substantial uncertainty keeps the parameter positive and reflects between-setting variability; mu and sigma are on the natural-log scale.
source: WHO-China Joint Mission Report on Coronavirus Disease 2019 (COVID-19), 2020

statement: For varicella in a susceptible population before routine vaccination, standard infectious-disease modeling references place the basic reproduction number roughly in the high single digits. A Normal prior centered at 9 with standard deviation 1.5 represents this transmission intensity while keeping nearly all mass in plausible positive values.
source: Anderson and May, Infectious Diseases of Humans: Dynamics and Control

statement: For early COVID-19 cases, the incubation period from infection or exposure to symptom onset was estimated with a median near 5 days and an upper tail around 11 to 12 days. A LogNormal prior with mu 1.63 and sigma 0.42 on the natural-log scale captures that empirical pattern in days.
source: Lauer et al., Annals of Internal Medicine, 2020 COVID-19 incubation-period study

statement: Seasonal influenza incubation is typically short, commonly described as about 1 to 4 days with an average near 2 days. A Gamma prior with alpha 16 and beta 8, using beta as the rate parameter, gives mean 2 days and standard deviation 0.5 days.

For measles, the incubation period from exposure to prodromal symptom onset is usually around 10 to 12 days, with recognized variation across cases. A LogNormal prior with median about 11 days and moderate dispersion provides a positive prior for exposure-to-symptom timing; mu and sigma are on the natural-log scale.
source: CDC Epidemiology and Prevention of Vaccine-Preventable Diseases (Pink Book), Measles chapter

statement: For Ebola virus disease, outbreak analyses and public-health references describe incubation as usually within 2 to 21 days, with a mean around 11 days in the West Africa outbreak data. A Gamma prior with alpha 5.2 and beta 0.456, using beta as the rate parameter, gives mean about 11.4 days and a broad right tail.
source: WHO Ebola Response Team, New England Journal of Medicine, 2014 Ebola outbreak study

statement: In a simple SIR-style influenza model, the recovery or removal rate can be anchored to an average contagious period of roughly 5 days. A Gamma prior with alpha 25 and beta 125, using beta as the rate parameter, has mean 0.20 day^-1 and standard deviation 0.04 day^-1.
source: CDC Influenza guidance on contagious period and transmission

statement: For measles, infectiousness is commonly described as extending from about 4 days before rash onset through 4 days after rash onset. Treating this as an approximately 8-day infectious period in a simple compartmental model gives a recovery or removal rate near 0.125 day^-1; a Gamma prior with alpha 25 and beta 200 encodes that scale.
source: CDC Epidemiology and Prevention of Vaccine-Preventable Diseases (Pink Book), Measles chapter

statement: Recent U.S. kindergarten two-dose MMR coverage is commonly reported near 93%, with state and year variation. A Beta prior with alpha 150.4 and beta 11.3 has mean about 0.93 and standard deviation about 0.02, making it a useful prior for aggregate school-entry MMR coverage as a proportion.
source: CDC SchoolVaxView and MMWR school vaccination coverage reports

statement: Global coverage with the third dose of diphtheria-tetanus-pertussis vaccine is often reported in the mid-80% range in recent WHO/UNICEF estimates. A Beta prior with alpha 69.7 and beta 13.3 has mean about 0.84 and standard deviation about 0.04, appropriate for a global aggregate coverage proportion.
source: WHO/UNICEF Estimates of National Immunization Coverage (WUENIC)
